images.alt: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. CDC twenty four seven. Saving Lives, Protecting People
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. CDC twenty four seven. Saving Lives, Protecting People
Graphic representation of the manual as a red book.
Circular graphic with center, middle, and outer rings displaying the standards, steps, and cross-cutting actions of the CDC Evaluation Framework.

Logic models help enhance the performance of the EHDI-IS. Strategies and outputs contribute to short-term, mid-term, and long-term outcomes. Strategies can be divided into four types: 1) System Enhancement could involve implementing a complete statewide EHDI-IS that conforms to the CDC EHDI Functional Standards, 2) Partnerships promote and support coordination and collaboration for jurisdictions’ tracking and surveillance activities, 3) Data Analysis and Evaluation leads strategic action for continuous project improvement, and 4) Data Use and Dissemination supports targeted dissemination of surveillance and evaluation findings for a variety of partner audiences. All these lead to outcomes, which can be short-term, mid-term, and long-term. Short-term outcomes include 1) enhanced jurisdictional EHDI-IS in accordance with the CDC EHDI Functional Standards, 2) increased collaboration between internal and external partners about sustained surveillance activities, 3) increased knowledge of current strengths and weaknesses of the EHDI-IS, and 4) increased dissemination of EHDI information that is accessible, timely, and actionable. Mid-term outcomes include 1) improved electronic exchange of data with other data systems, 2) increased simplicity and stability of the EHDI-IS, 3) increased number of providers and/or provider sites consistently reporting screening, diagnostic, and early intervention data to the EHDI program, 4) improved documentation of high-quality data which is accurate, complete, consistent, on time, unique, and valid for the three stages of the EHDI process, and 5) improved program planning, policy development, and decision making to support tracking and surveillance activities. The main long-term outcome is a useful EHDI-IS in place that conforms to the CDC Functional Standards to support 1) tracking for infants and young children throughout the EHDI process and 2) connecting deaf and hard of hearing infants with needed services.

images.alt: Data Quality Table defining five attributes: Accuracy (the extent that data are correct, reliable, and certified free of error); Completeness (the proportion of stored data against the potential of "100% complete"); Consistency (the absence of difference, when comparing two or more representations of a thing against a definition); Uniqueness (nothing will be recorded more than once based upon how that thing is identified); Validity [data are valid if it conforms to the syntax (format, type, range) of its definition].

images.alt: Diagram showing the Common Ways to Collect Data for Evaluation Purposes, which are questionnaire (with an image of a notepad and paper), observation (with an image of a magnifying glass), database (with an image of a chart), focus groups (with an image of a group of people), interview (with an image of people at opposite ends of a table), and document review (with an image of a magnifying glass over a piece of paper).

og.title: EHDI Guidance Manual – Chapter 6: Monitoring and Evaluation
og.description: To educate the audience about the importance of developing and implementing an EDHI evaluation plan.
